title: Issue Distribution by Device and Site
dataSources: `index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:issue` from the `issue` modular input in `TA_cisco_catalyst` (Splunkbase 7538). Same data feed as UC-5.13.21. Key fields for distribution: `deviceId` or `deviceName` (identifies the affected device), `siteId` (identifies the site), `name` (issue title for breadth analysis).
spl:
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue" status!="RESOLVED"
| stats dc(issueId) as unique_issues dc(name) as issue_types by deviceName, siteId
| lookup catalyst_site_lookup siteId OUTPUT siteName
| eval site_label=coalesce(siteName, siteId)
| sort -unique_issues
| head 20
description: Maps active Assurance issues to specific devices and sites, ranking by issue concentration to identify problem hotspots — the noisiest devices and the most troubled locations — so operations sends engineers to the exact trouble spots instead of guessing where to look.
value: UC-5.13.21 tells you what issues exist; this UC tells you *where they cluster*. A device with 15 unique issues is either failing catastrophically or misconfigured. A site with 40 issues across 10 devices has a systemic infrastructure problem (power, cooling, upstream connectivity). Ranking by concentration focuses remediation budget on the highest-impact locations and devices. The `issue_types` dimension distinguishes a device with 15 instances of one issue (stutter — likely a polling artifact or recurring transient) from a device with 15 different issue types (broad failure — likely failing hardware).
implementation: Same `issue` input as UC-5.13.21. Use `dc(issueId)` for unique issue count and `dc(name)` for issue type breadth. Enrich with `catalyst_site_lookup` for human-readable site names. Schedule weekly for the operations review.
visualization: (1) Table: deviceName, site_label, unique_issues, issue_types — top 20, sorted by unique_issues descending. Drilldown: click a device → filter UC-5.13.21 to that device. (2) Treemap: site (parent) → device (child), sized by unique_issues. (3) Scatter: unique_issues (x) vs issue_types (y) — devices in the top-right (many issues, many types) need immediate attention. (4) Bar chart: top 10 sites by total unique_issues.

references.title: Catalyst Center Intent API — Issues endpoint

knownFalsePositives:
**Single device with a persistent issue inflating that device's issue count.** A device with an unresolved bug or configuration issue will generate an issue event on every poll, making it appear as the top issue generator. Distinguish by checking whether the high `unique_issues` for a device corresponds to many distinct `issueId` values or the same ID re-appearing. `dc(issueId)` already handles this — it counts unique issues, not poll events.

**Lab or PoC devices generating issues that are not operationally relevant.** Test equipment may generate issues that inflate the per-device distribution. Distinguish by checking `deviceName` against lab naming conventions. Suppress with a `catalyst_excluded_devices` lookup.

**Site hierarchy change reassigning devices and issues to different siteId values.** When the site hierarchy is reorganised, issues for the same device appear under a different `siteId`. Distinguish by correlating with `index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs"` for hierarchy changes. Suppress by tracking by `deviceName` (stable) rather than `siteId` (may change).

**Cross-product issues showing the correlating device as the source.** Issues detected through cross-product correlation (e.g., ISE authentication failure) may be attributed to a device rather than the external system. Distinguish by checking `category` — cross-product issues often have specific category names. Do not suppress — the attribution is correct but the root cause may be in the external system.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• UC-5.13.21 (Issue Summary) must be operational — same `issue` data feed.
• `deviceName` (or `deviceId`) must be populated in issue events. Some issue types (global/control-plane issues) may not have a device association — these will appear with null `deviceName`. Handle with `| eval deviceName=coalesce(deviceName, "(no device)")` if needed.
• `siteId` populated for site-level distribution. Use `catalyst_site_lookup` for human-readable site names (UC-5.13.5 or UC-5.13.51).

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Same `issue` input as UC-5.13.21. No additional configuration.

Step 2 — Create the search and report
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue" status!="RESOLVED"
| stats dc(issueId) as unique_issues dc(name) as issue_types by deviceName, siteId
| lookup catalyst_site_lookup siteId OUTPUT siteName
| eval site_label=coalesce(siteName, siteId)
| sort -unique_issues
| head 20
```

Why `dc(issueId)` not `count`: `count` inflates with poll frequency (same issue counted every 15 minutes). `dc(issueId)` gives the actual number of distinct active issues per device — the operationally meaningful metric.

Why `dc(name) as issue_types`: distinguishes concentrated vs distributed problems. `unique_issues=15, issue_types=1` = one persistent issue re-detected across polls (stutter). `unique_issues=15, issue_types=12` = many different problems (broad failure, likely hardware).

Why `status != "RESOLVED"`: focuses on active issues. Including resolved issues would show historical hotspots (useful for trend analysis — see variant below).

Variant — historical hotspot analysis (which devices generated the most issues over 30 days, including resolved):
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue" earliest=-30d
| stats dc(issueId) as total_issues dc(name) as issue_types dc(eval(if(status="RESOLVED",issueId,null()))) as resolved by deviceName
| eval unresolved=total_issues-resolved
| sort -total_issues
| head 20
```

For site-level distribution (which sites have the most issues):
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue" status!="RESOLVED"
| stats dc(issueId) as site_issues dc(deviceName) as affected_devices by siteId
| lookup catalyst_site_lookup siteId OUTPUT siteName
| sort -site_issues
| head 20
```

Schedule as Report: weekly (cron `0 7 * * 1`). Schedule the site variant monthly for regional operations.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Pick the top device from the results. Open **Catalyst Center > Assurance > Issues** and filter by that device. The issue count should match `unique_issues` within one poll cycle.

(b) Verify `issue_types` makes sense: a device with `unique_issues=5, issue_types=5` has 5 different problems. A device with `unique_issues=5, issue_types=1` has one issue detected 5 different times (check if these are truly unique `issueId` values or re-detections).

(c) Cross-reference with UC-5.13.1 (Device Health): do the top issue-generators also have the lowest health scores? If yes, the data is consistent. If a device has many issues but high health, investigate — the issues may be informational (P4) rather than impactful.

(d) Vendor UI parity: open **Catalyst Center > Assurance > Issues** and sort by device. The top devices should appear in both views.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard placement (on the "Issue Triage" dashboard or a dedicated "Issue Hotspots" dashboard):
• Table: top 20 devices by unique_issues, with site_label, issue_types. Drilldown to UC-5.13.21 filtered by `deviceName`.
• Treemap: sites as parent rectangles, devices as child rectangles, sized by unique_issues.
• Scatter plot: unique_issues vs issue_types per device — top-right quadrant is highest priority.

Runbook (owner: Operations, weekly review):
1. Review the top 20 noisiest devices.
2. For devices with high `unique_issues` AND high `issue_types` (many distinct problems): these are failing broadly. Check hardware health, firmware version, and configuration. Consider RMA.
3. For devices with high `unique_issues` but low `issue_types` (one recurring problem): this is a persistent issue. Assign to Problem Management (UC-5.13.25).
4. For the top 5 noisiest sites: check whether issues concentrate on one device (device problem) or spread across many devices (site problem — power, cooling, upstream).
5. Track week-over-week: are the same devices at the top? If yes, remediation isn't working.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **`deviceName` is null for many issues** — some Assurance issue types (global, control-plane) aren't tied to a specific device. Group nulls into a `(no device)` bucket: `| eval deviceName=coalesce(deviceName, "(no device)")`.

• **Same device appears at the top every week** — the device has chronic unresolved issues. This is the intended signal — escalate to Problem Management.

• **`siteId` is null** — devices not assigned to a site in Catalyst Center. Assign them or filter nulls.

• **Issue count much higher in Splunk than the GUI** — `dc(issueId)` across a long time range captures issues that may have been resolved and re-opened. Narrow to `status != "RESOLVED"` for the active snapshot.

• **Treemap is too dense** — with 200+ sites and 5,000 devices, the visualisation is overwhelming. Filter to `| head 20` for the top devices, or use the site-level variant for a coarser view.

• **Device with high issue count but no user complaints** — the issues may be P4 informational or affect a low-traffic area. Check `priority` distribution for that device.

• **Search is slow** — narrow to `earliest=-24h` for the active snapshot. Use summary indexing for monthly historical hotspot analysis.

• **Cross-product issues inflate device counts** — an ISE failure may generate onboarding issues for dozens of devices. The root cause is ISE, not the devices. Check `category="Onboarding"` and correlate with UC-5.13.14.
grandmaExplanation: We rank the noisiest network devices and the buildings they sit in by how many problems each one has. This tells your team exactly which devices and which locations need attention first, instead of guessing where the trouble is.
